scene_title: The same. The field of battle

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Ride, ride, Messala, ride, and give these bills

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Unto the legions on the other side. Loud alarum.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Let them set on at once; for I perceive

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: But cold demeanour in Octavius’ wing,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And sudden push gives them the overthrow.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Ride, ride, Messala: let them all come down.

glosses.definition: a call to arms; a trumpet signal for battle
lack of aggression; cold fighting spirit

annotations.title: Six lines: the shortest scene in the play
annotations.body:
Brutus's hurried order to Messala — attack Octavius's wing, which is showing 'cold demeanour' — is six lines. It is Shakespeare's device for the speed of battle. Where 4.3 was a 300-line tent scene in which philosophical positions were debated and relationships tested, 5.2 is the battlefield itself: commands given, riders dispatched, alarums within. Shakespeare had used this technique before (the short battlefield scenes in *Henry IV Part 1* at Shrewsbury) and would again (Agincourt in *Henry V*), but the contrast between the long rhetorical setup of 5.1 and the four-word lines of 5.2 is especially sharp. Political oratory has ended; killing has begun.

The historical note is that Brutus's perception is correct: Octavius's wing did show weakness at the first battle of Philippi, and Brutus's forces temporarily overran Octavius's camp. But Cassius's wing, attacked by Antony, broke; Cassius killed himself on the mistaken report of Titinius's death. The first battle was a tactical draw; the second, weeks later, destroyed Brutus's army.

options: Stagecraft — after the long rhetorical 5.1, the six-line scene signals that talk has stopped and killing has begun; battlefield speed as dramatic compression
